concepts.title: Anti Markovnikov addition
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction Of Alkenes

(1) Addition Reaction

Rules governing adding reaction.

 (a) Markonikov addition

Mechanism for the addition of hydrogen halide to an alkene involves the following two steps:

The above addition follows Markonikov’s rule . It states that in the addition of HX to an alkene, the hydrogen atom adds to the carbon atom of the double bond that has the greater number of hydrogen atoms or the negative part of reagent adds to carbon having less number of hydrogen.

For example

The addition of $\text{HBr}$ to some alkenes gives a mixture of the expected alkyl bromide and an isomer formed by rearrangement.

(b) Anti-markonikov’s rule :

When addition of $\text{HBr}$ takes place in presence of peroxides ( $\text{R}-\text{O}-\text{O}-\text{R}$ or benzoyl peroxide), addition occurs in an anti-Markonikov’s manner i.e., $\text{Br}$ is added to the carbon having large number of $\text{H}$-atom.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CH}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{HBr}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ROOR}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Br}$

Mechanism 

Example 

Why does only Bromine gives anti-Markonikov’s addition :

Solution: 

Only for HBr, both the steps are exothermic while for $\text{HF}$ the second step is highly endothermic, reflecting the strength of $\text{H}-\text{F}$ bond and the difficulty of breaking it. For $\text{HCl}$, it is again the second step that is endothermic while for $\text{HI}$, it is the first step that is endothermic, reflecting the fact that the energy gained in forming the weak $\text{I}-\text{C}$ bond is not as much as that lost in breaking the $\text{C}=\text{C}\pi $ bond.

 (a) SYN

(cis type same side)

(b) ANTI

(trans type $\therefore $ opp side)

results in trans product.

concepts.title: Pseudo First Order Reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Pseudo First Order Reaction

There are circumstances where a second order reaction might appear, in an experiment, to be first order. That is when one of the reactants in the rate equation is present in great excess over the other in the reaction mixture.

As an example, let’s look again at the mixed second order rate law that we treated in the last section.

We had the rate law, $\frac{\text{d}[\text{B}]}{\text{dt}}=-\text{k}[\text{B}][\text{A}]\text{,}$ with a reaction whose stoichiometry is, $2~\text{A}+\text{B}\to \text{P}+\text{ etc}$

$(\text{P}=\text{ product})$

Sometimes ${{[\text{A}]}_{0}}>>{{[\text{B}]}_{0}}$.

For example, say we prepare the system such that,

${{[\text{A}]}_{0}}\text{ }=2.0\text{M and}$

${{[\text{B}]}_{0}}=0.001\text{M}$

Then, when the reaction has run to completion, (at time= infinity),

${{[\text{A}]}_{\text{inf }}}={{[\text{A}]}_{0}}-2{{[~\text{B}]}_{0}}=2.0-0.0001=1.998\approx 2.0$

So $[\text{A}]$ is approximately constant throughout the entire reaction.

Then the rate law becomes,

$\frac{\text{d}[\text{B}]}{\text{dt}}=-\text{k}[\text{B}][\text{A}]\approx -\text{k}[\text{B}]{{[\text{A}]}_{0}}$ …(1)

which is effectively a first order rate reaction. This rate equation has the solution,

$[\text{B}]={{[\text{B}]}_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\text{k}{{[\text{A}]}_{0}}}}$ … (2)

where the “effective” first order rate constant is $\text{k}{{[\text{A}]}_{0}}$.

You can actually solve this problem as a mixed second order rate problem, as we did in the last section, but it is lot more work.

Pseudo first order reactions are sometimes used to find the rate constant of a second order reaction when one of your two components is very expensive and the other one is relatively cheap. You can use an excess of the inexpensive reagent and use a small amount of the expensive one. Notice that you can still obtain the second order rate constant by dividing the effective first order rate constant by the concentration of the excess component.

concepts.title: Catalytic hydrogenation
concepts.conceptBody:
Addition Reactions

 Catalytic Hydrogenation:

Alkenes react with hydrogen in the presence of many reagents such as:

(1) Na in liquid $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ and alcohol

(2) ${{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{Pt}{{\text{O}}_{2}}/\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$

(3) ${{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{Ni}$ at $573~\text{K}$

(4) ${{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{Pd}$-carbon in ethanol

It is a ‘ syn-addition ’ reaction.

Reducing Agents

- ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$, Ni or Pd or Pt $300\text{C}$

- ${{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{Pt}{{\text{O}}_{2}}/\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ exclusive for alkenes cannot reduce

$\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{N} \\ \text{O} \\ \text{X} \\ \end{array} \right]$

$-\quad {{\text{H}}_{2}}/\frac{\text{Pd}-\text{C}}{\text{ (methanol) or ethanol }}$

Note:

- $\quad \text{Pt}{{\text{O}}_{2}}/\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$ is used exclusively for alkenes ( $\equiv $ also)

- $\quad \text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\to $ reduces all except alkenes

$\text{NaB}{{\text{H}}_{4}}/\text{OH}-$

concepts.title: Oxidation of alkenes
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidation of Alkenes

1. Oxidation by ${{\text{O}}_{3}}$ (ozone)

2. Oxidation by hot acidic $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

Ozonolysis:

Ozone reacts vigorously with alkene to form unstable compound called molozonide, which rearranges spontaneously to form a compound known as ozonide. Ozonides, themselves are unstable and reduced directly with $\text{Zn}$ and water. The reduction produces carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones) that can be isolated.

  • Overall transformation: $\text{C}=\text{C}$ to $2\quad \text{C}=\text{O}$
  • Ozonolysis implies that ozone causes the alkene to break (-lysis)
  • a reducing work-up, either $\text{Zn}$ in acetic acid or dimethyl sulfide, ${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}~\text{S}$
  • an oxidising work-up, usually ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (under these conditions, carboxylic acids are obtained instead of aldehydes)
  • It is convenient to view the process as cleaving the alkene into two carbonyls
  • The substituents on the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ depend on the substituents on the original $\text{C}=\text{C}$
  • The work-up determines the oxidation state of the products

Reductive work-up

Oxidative work-up

concepts.title: Reaction with carbenes
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction with Carbenes

For carbenes generation we need a source of carbene

We use $C{{H}_{2}}{{N}_{2}}$ (diazomethane) or $C{{H}_{2}}=C=O$ (Ketene)

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}~{{\text{N}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[{}]{\Delta }\underset{\text{ (carbene) }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}:+{{\text{N}}_{2}}}}\,$

  • Carbene is electron deficient. It adds across the double bond.

Mechanism :

Step 1: Attack on double bond

Addition in Syn

  • In presence of potassium tert-butoxide, there is addition of dichloromethylene (dichlorocarbone),  $:CC{{l}_{2}},$ when $\text{CHC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$reacts with alkene.

first: $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is formed by $\alpha $-elimination $\left( 1,1 \right.$-elimination) from $\text{CHC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ and $\text{t}-\text{BuOH}$
